constraints: Sum of |S| over all test cases <= 5*10^6
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a string S consisting only of characters '<' and '>'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the length of the longest prefix of S that forms a correct sequence where every '<' is matched with a later '>' and no '>' appears without a preceding unmatched '<'. If no such prefix exists, print 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For "<<>>" the whole string is balanced, length 4. "<><>" becomes balanced after each pair, final length 4. "<>>>" becomes balanced after the first two characters, then a stray '>' makes the prefix invalid, so longest valid prefix length is 2.

First string starts with '>' – no valid prefix. Second string "<><" returns to balance zero after the first two characters, so the longest valid prefix length is 2. Third string "<<>>><" is balanced up to the fourth character, after that an extra '>' breaks the sequence; longest valid prefix length is 4.
All eight characters form a correct sequence, therefore the answer is 8.

solution.approach: The key insight is to maintain a balance counter that increments for each '<' and decrements for each '>'. The sequence is valid as long as the balance is non-negative. The algorithm iterates through the string, updating the balance and checking for validity. The time complexity is O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string, because each character is visited once.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T*N) total, where N is the length of the string and T is the number of test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space used does not grow with the size of the input, only a constant amount of space is used to store the balance and the best prefix length.
